one_line_takeaway: A single suffix-token edit crafted purely on a surrogate LLM, with no query, corpus, or victim-model access, can measurably suppress a target document's retrieval rank across multiple real LLM-based retrievers, and no tested defense (perplexity filters, reranking, built-in truncation) stops it.
key_finding: On average across the 4 BRIGHT datasets and 7 victim retrievers, DQ-A achieves the largest performance drops among all compared methods on 6 of 7 retrievers, with roughly 2-10% absolute drop in Recall@25 and 1-7% in Recall@50 on average; on the Robotics dataset specifically, DQ-A drives up to an 8-point Recall@50 drop for Qwen1.5-7B-Instruct and 6-point for Jina-Embeddings-v3 -- about a 30% and 20% relative reduction from those retrievers' original performance. Concretely (Table 2, Robotics, R@50): Qwen1.5-7B-Ins drops from 0.311 (original) to 0.231 under DQ-A vs. 0.259 (GCG), 0.268 (PRADA), 0.399 (Poisoned-RAG, which is actually gray-flagged as ineffective/counterproductive). Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B is the one retriever where neither DQ-A nor any baseline achieves a meaningful drop, which the authors attribute to possible post-training against injection attacks. Poisoned-RAG frequently increases rather than decreases retrieval performance despite being prompted to diminish the document -- attributed to the generation task's inherent relevance bias. Ablations (Table 3) show DQ-A-H (no word-embedding trick) still generally outperforms S-GCG (no adversarial learning at all), indicating the adversarial-learning mechanism is the primary source of effectiveness, with the word-embedding-surrogate trick as a secondary refinement (DQ-A slightly beats DQ-A-H). Injected token length (10 vs 20) has only a small effect on attack strength; injection position matters more, with middle-of-document injection being markedly weaker than beginning or end (attributed to transformer attention patterns). The attack survives a retriever+reranker pipeline (Table 4) because it operates at the retrieval stage, filtering the document out of the candidate pool before the reranker ever sees it. Per-document attack cost is reported as ~23.065s with delta=10, 20 epochs (Table 5), with time complexity O(|S|(|d|+delta)^2 * D_dim + delta*V). The authors state no existing defense (corpus-poisoning detectors, perplexity-based token-injection detectors, or built-in truncation preprocessing in Gemma/Jina) was found effective against this attack.
core_contribution: The paper proposes DQ-A (Document-Queries Adversarial learning), a query-agnostic, complete black-box token-injection attack that hides a target document from LLM-based retrievers (LLMR). Unlike prior injection attacks, the attacker has no access to the victim model's parameters or queries, no query access at attack time, and no view of the document corpus -- only the ability to append a small number of suffix tokens to the document itself (simulating a normal user edit, e.g. a Wikipedia contributor or Reddit commenter). The method: (1) formalizes an 'epsilon-p_epsilon-Precise Retriever' definition capturing the empirically observed phenomenon that LLM retriever embeddings cluster by topic; (2) proves a transferability theorem (Theorem 3.2) relating optimization on a surrogate retriever g to guaranteed similarity decrease on an unknown victim retriever f; (3) turns the attack into a min-max problem solved via adversarial learning between the perturbed document and a population of LLM-sampled candidate queries (using a 'casual' i.e. causal/generic LLM, e.g. Qwen-1.5-4B-Casual, to sample N diverse candidate queries related to the document); (4) uses Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) to optimize injected suffix tokens against the maximum similarity among sampled queries, and optimizes the sampled queries themselves toward the document; (5) empirically finds that running the adversarial optimization against the surrogate model's word-embedding layer (rather than its last-hidden retrieval embedding) produces a more transferable attack.
threat_model: Complete black-box attacker who: (1) does not know or query the victim retriever model (no architecture/parameter access, no query interface); (2) does not know the user's query, before or at attack time; (3) has no access to the document corpus held by the retrieval system; (4) can only make a small, suffix-only edit to the single victim document, bounded by an injected-token-count budget delta (default 10 tokens in main experiments). This is explicitly a 'diminish/hide' threat model (make a document harder to retrieve), not a poisoning/boosting threat model, though the Discussion section notes the loss can in principle be reversed for a boosting variant (tested only in a small preliminary experiment, not the paper's main claim).
limitations: (1) The attack's core mechanism relies on pushing the document's embedding out of its topic cluster ('rejecting' it from relevant queries); the authors' own Discussion section states this makes it poorly suited to the 'boost within relevant queries with higher ranking' goal -- only the narrower 'boost against an irrelevant query' variant was shown to work, and only in a small preliminary test (Recall@50 raised from 0.00227 to 0.00601, NDCG@50 from 0.00116 to 0.00251 -- effect sizes near zero in absolute terms). (2) Comparisons against baselines are explicitly imbalanced: baselines get the ground-truth query and (for PRADA) 10% of the query/document set for LoRA finetuning, while DQ-A gets neither -- a favorable-to-baselines setup the authors call out themselves. (3) The epsilon-p_epsilon-Precise Retriever definition and Theorem 3.2 are only 'approximately' verified empirically, restricting the theoretically infinite topic context set to a countable one containing just the victim document and query (full derivation in Appendix A.2, not extracted here). (4) On Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B, neither the proposed attack nor baselines achieve meaningful degradation, suggesting the method's effectiveness is retriever-dependent and not universal. (5) The paper studies text-only, embedding-based dense retrieval; no evaluation against sparse/BM25 retrieval, multi-hop RAG generation pipelines, or production search systems with additional preprocessing/defenses beyond the two reranker combos and truncation-preprocessing tested.

datasets_used: BRIGHT benchmark: economics, psychology, biology, robotics splits
quantitative_results: Average Recall@25 drop 2-10% and Recall@50 drop 1-7% across 4 BRIGHT datasets on 6 of 7 victim retrievers (Table 2)
Robotics dataset: up to 8-point Recall@50 drop for Qwen1.5-7B-Instruct and 6-point drop for Jina-Embeddings-v3, ~30% and ~20% relative reduction from original performance
Qwen1.5-7B-Ins, Robotics, R@50: Original 0.311 -> DQ-A 0.231 vs GCG 0.259, PRADA 0.268, Poisoned-RAG 0.399 (Table 2)
Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B: no method (DQ-A or baselines) achieves a significant attack effect
DQ-A-H outperforms S-GCG ablation on most metrics (Table 3), validating adversarial learning design over plain GCG-with-sampled-queries
|S| sweep 5-15 samples: performance drop increases from |S|=5 to 10, then partially reverses from 10 to 15 (Figure 5)
Injected token length swept 10-20: only slight variation in attack effect (Figure 6)
Injection position ablation on Gemma: middle-of-document injection is weakest, beginning and end are stronger (Figure 7)
Retriever+reranker pipelines (BGE-M3+BGE-reranker-v2-M3; Embedding-Gemma-300M+Qwen3-reranker-0.6B) still show NDCG25/50/100 drops under DQ-A (Table 4)
Reversed (boosting) variant on a single re-targeted document: Recall@50 raised from 0.00227 to 0.00601 and NDCG@50 from 0.00116 to 0.00251 (Discussion section, small preliminary test)
Per-document attack cost: ~23.065s average with delta=10, 20 epochs; complexity O(|S|(|d|+delta)^2 * D_dim + delta*V) (Table 5)
baselines_compared: GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient, per Kumar & Lakkaraju 2024) on the same surrogate model, given ground-truth query
PRADA (Wu et al., 2023) on the same surrogate model, given ground-truth query plus 10% query/document data for LoRA alignment finetuning
Poisoned-RAG (Zou et al., 2025), prompted to diminish the document, given ground-truth query
DQ-A-H (ablation: DQ-A without word-embedding-surrogate strategy, uses last-hidden instead)
S-GCG (ablation: plain GCG with sampled initial queries and summed similarity loss, no adversarial learning)
